title: Divine Imagination and the Intermediate World: Ibn Arabi on the Barzakh — v6
author: James W. Morris

body.text: Barzakh. The word means barrier, isthmus, something that separates two things without becoming either of them. Ibn Arabi turns this ordinary Arabic word into the name for divine Imagination itself. The intermediate realm between sensory and intelligible — ontological, a region of being beyond geography. The faculty through which all spiritual perception becomes possible.

For centuries after the time of the Andalusian mystic Ibn Arabi, his remarkable discussions of the Imagination were elaborated by Islamic philosophers, poets, artists and critics in order to explain, interpret and justify the full range of artistic and creative activities carried on within later Islamicate cultures, as far away as India and Indonesia. Another major source of those classical Islamic understandings of the Imagination was in the Shaykh's many discussions of the eschatological Barzakh or intermediate world of the divine Imagination, as well as his accounts of his own striking experiences and decisive spiritual encounters there, which are scattered throughout his magnum opus, al-Futuhat al-Makkiya.

body.caption: The Barzakh: what separates two things while partaking of both.

body.text: Ibn Arabi's method is deliberate disorientation. The constant shifts between perspectives, images, and technical vocabularies break down the ordinary dualities — subject and object, divine and human, spiritual and material — that keep you from seeing what he is pointing at. The aim is transformation through disorientation.

Even the most cursory reading of this chapter would make quickly evident that Ibn Arabi's intention was not to clarify in any sort of rational, conceptual form the different ways we can speak of the Imagination. The recurrent mixture of and sudden shifts between a whole spectrum of contrasting approaches, images, perspectives and technical vocabularies is not accidental but instead forms a complex, highly self-conscious rhetorical technique. His ultimate aim is not simply heightened intellectual understanding, but to bring about in the properly prepared reader a suddenly transformed state of immediate realization in which each of the implicit dualities of our usual perception of things is directly transcended in an enlightened moment of unitive vision. His aim is to bring his readers to see everything as ongoing theophany and ever-renewed divine creation.

Ibn Arabi's definition of Barzakh sounds simple until you sit with it. What separates two things without ever becoming either of them. The line between shadow and sunlight. The divider between two seas that keeps them from mixing. You cannot see the divider with your eyes, but your intellect judges that something must be there. That something, grasped only by the intellect, is the Barzakh.

body.caption: Ibn Arabi's rhetorical technique: writing to transform.

body.text: Know that the word 'barzakh' is an expression for what separates two things without ever becoming either of them, such as the line separating a shadow from the sunlight. Even if our senses are unable to perceive what separates those two things, the intellect judges that there is indeed a divider separating them, and that divider grasped by the intellect is precisely the barzakh. Because if something is perceived by the senses, it must be one of those two things, rather than the barzakh. Each of those two things, when they are adjacent to each other, have need of a barzakh which is not the same as each of them, but which has in itself the power of each of them.

Now the crucial move: the Barzakh has the power of both sides. It participates in both realities while being reducible to neither. That makes it the perfect description of Imagination. Imagination partakes of the spiritual and the material, the real and the apparent, the known and the unknown. It is neither, and both.

body.caption: The Barzakh as the separator that has the power of both sides.

body.text: Now since the Barzakh of the creative divine Imagination is something separating what is knowable and unknowable, existent and non-existent, intelligible and unintelligible, affirmed and negated, it has been given the name Barzakh as a technical term. It is intelligible in itself, yet it is nothing but the imagined-image. For when you perceive it, assuming you are in a rational state, you know that you have perceived something existent on which your gaze has fallen. You most definitely know that there is absolutely something there. But what is this about which you affirm that it is an existent thing, while at the same time you are also denying that? For this Imagination-Image is neither entirely existent nor non-existent, neither entirely known nor unknowable, neither entirely affirmed nor denied.

Your reflection in a mirror. You know you have perceived your own form. You also know the form you see is not really you — it is smaller, reversed, exists in a different way. Both affirmed and denied, both existent and non-existent. If you cannot fully grasp the reality of a mirror reflection — something you see every day — how much less can you grasp the divine self-manifestations that come through the same imaginal mode?

body.caption: The mirror analogy: both affirmed and denied.

body.text: This is like a human being perceiving their reflected form in the mirror. The person definitely knows that they have perceived their own form in a certain respect, while they know just as absolutely that they have not perceived their form in another respect, because of the smallness of the image they see. What is that reflected form? And where is it actually located? And what is its ontological status? For it is both affirmed and denied, both existent and non-existent, both known and unknown. Now God has made this reality appear to His servants so that they might know that if they are bewildered and incapable of grasping the reality of this phenomenon which is only part of this world, then how much more incapable they must be regarding the Creator of that reality. In this way God has pointed out that the divine Self-manifestations to them are even more subtle and delicate than this case in which their intellects are already so bewildered.

Two eyes: physical sensation and imagination. The same object can be perceived through either. How do you know which eye is active? Watch the object. If a chameleon shifts color as you watch it, and your watching does not arrest the shift, you are seeing with the eye of imagination. The imagination moves with its object, participates in its flow.

body.caption: The two eyes: physical sensation and imagination.

body.text: There are some people who perceive this imaged-object with the eye of physical sensation, and there are others who perceive it with the eye of imagination. If a person wishes to distinguish between those two modes of perception in their waking state, they can determine it by looking at the imaged-object. Thus if the states of what one is looking at continue to change as it changes its shape and characteristics, even though you cannot deny that it is still the same thing, and the fact of observing it does not stop it from changing its shape and formation, as when a chameleon when you observe it stops changing its colors, then that is undoubtedly being seen with the eye of imagination, not with the eye of the senses.

When people claim to see spirits, angels, or God in a dream, they see with the eye of imagination. The vision is real — real in the mode of the Barzakh. The judgment about what the vision means belongs to the intellect, and only the interpretation can be mistaken. Ibn Arabi is clear on this.

Few of those who lay claim to the unveiled vision of spirits really understand how this is. When those spirits take on the image of perceptible forms, most people do not know whether they are perceiving them with the eye of the imagination or with the eye of physical sensation. This is how you should understand how a human being can perceive their Lord in a dream, even though He transcends any form or image. So you should know with which eye you are seeing Him! For I have already let you know that the divine Imagination is perceived either through itself, through the eye of imagination, or through physical vision.

body.caption: The Horn of Light: the eschatological Barzakh.

body.text: The Horn. In Arabic, al-sur means both 'horn' and is the plural of 'form.' The Horn the angel Israfil blows at the Resurrection is simultaneously the realm of forms where souls exist after death. Made of light, because light is what makes things visible. This Horn is the Barzakh itself. The pun is the whole theology compressed into a single word.

The prescriber of Revelation called this thing, which is the divine Presence of the Barzakh to which we are brought after death and in which we directly witness our souls, a Horn and Trumpet. Here the word al-sur is also the plural of the word sura, 'form.' Know that the Messenger of God, when he was asked what this Horn was, replied: 'It is a horn of Light upon which the angel Israfil blows.' The breadth of this Horn of the Barzakh-Imagination is as broad as possible, since none of the generated things are broader than It. That is because through Its reality It has sway over every thing, and even over what is not a thing. It gives form alike to absolute nothingness and to what is ontologically impossible, necessary and contingent, turning what is existent into nothingness and what is non-existent into being.

The shape reverses expectation. The narrow end is at the top, the wide end at the bottom. The highest spiritual realities — the divine Essence — are the most concentrated, the most narrow. The lower you descend toward multiplicity, the wider the realm becomes. The path of spiritual knowledge is a movement from width to narrowness, from many objects of knowledge to the One.

body.caption: The narrow and the wide: the path from multiplicity to unity.

body.text: Since the Imagination gives form to God as well as everything in the world below Him, its highest section is the narrow one and its lowest section is the wide one. That is just how God created It: for the first thing He created from It was narrow, while what He created from It later widened out. When the true Knowers want to proceed to knowing the Unicity of God, they keep on ascending gradually from that breadth toward what is narrower. Their forms of knowing keep on becoming less numerous while they ascend in their knowledge of Gods Essence, until they reach the point where they have no object of knowledge but God alone, which is the narrowest point in that Horn.

After death, spirits are deposited in imaginal-bodily forms within this Horn. They perceive through the eye and light of the form they inhabit. This is why every dream is true — the interpretation can be mistaken, but the dream itself is always real. The Prophet understood this when a man said his head was cut off and continued speaking: the man saw something real. The mistake was in what he thought it meant.

body.caption: Dreams and the Barzakh: every dream is true.

body.text: When God takes the spirits from these physical bodies wherever they may be at death, He deposits those spirits in imaginal-bodily forms within this Horn of Light. All of those things that the human being perceives after death in the Barzakh are only perceived through the eye and with the light of the form in which that person exists in that Horn, and that is a true perception. This is why one's dreams are always true, because every dream in itself is true and not in error. Because if we say a dream was mistaken, it is not really the dream itself that was mistaken, but rather the person who interpreted it incorrectly, in that they did not recognize the intended meaning of the image in question.

The imagination always perceives truly, without passing judgment. Error belongs only to the intellect's interpretation. Every image, every vision, every dream is real in the mode of the Barzakh. The spiritual task is to learn to interpret correctly what is already real. That shifts the whole ground of spiritual practice.

As for our companions, they were mistaken in their thinking about this Horn. They considered the wide segment of this Horn its highest part and its narrow section the lowest part of the world, but things are not at all as they claimed. The imagination perceives with its light whatever it perceives, without passing judgment. The judgment only belongs to something else, which is the intellect, so the error cannot be attributed to the imagination. Thus there never is any false imagination at all, indeed all of it is correct. Every human being in the Barzakh is hostage to what they have acquired, imprisoned in the forms of their deeds, until they are raised up from those forms on the Day of the Rising.
